nativeRange.habitat: Shallow coastal streams, tidal creeks, estuaries, lagoons, and swampy backwaters; often among aquatic vegetation or overhanging riparian growth. Estuarine and coastal-plain populations tolerate fluctuating salinity; upland freshwater populations remain in pure freshwater.

decorPreferences: dense fine-leafed planting (Java moss, hornwort, water sprite)
floating plants for surface cover and egg-laying substrate
dark substrate to enhance colour and reduce shyness
open midwater swimming lanes between planting zones
gentle flow — low to moderate filtration output

diet.notes: Micro-predator in the wild; feeds on small invertebrates, zooplankton, mosquito larvae, and tiny flying insects at the surface. In the aquarium, accepts high-quality micro-pellets, crushed flake, and small frozen or live foods such as baby brine shrimp, daphnia, micro-worms, and grindal worms. Small mouth size limits food particle diameter — standard flake should be crushed before offering. Multiple small feedings per day are preferred over a single large meal.

goodWith: other small rainbowfishes (Melanotaenia boesemani juveniles, Pseudomugil gertrudae)
non-predatory dwarf cichlids such as Apistogramma kept with caution
freshwater shrimp (adults safe; fry may be taken)
avoidWith: any fish large enough to swallow them whole
boisterous fish that out-compete them at feeding

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species page image of Pseudomugil signifer male showing iridescent blue eye. Standard FishBase contributed photo.
Wikimedia Commons image of P. signifer in aquarium showing characteristic blue eye and finnage. Verify licence and attribution before use.

summary: The Pacific Blue-Eye (Pseudomugil signifer) is a small, peaceful nano fish from coastal eastern Australia, named for its vivid iridescent blue iris. At 3–4 cm, it is among the most accessible Pseudomugil species for beginners, tolerating cooler temperatures and mild brackish conditions.
careNotes:
The Pacific Blue-Eye is native to coastal eastern Australia from the Sydney Basin north to Cape York Peninsula, occurring in freshwater streams, tidal creeks, and estuaries. Its defining feature is a vivid iridescent blue iris that gleams under aquarium lighting. Adults reach only 3–4 cm, placing them firmly in the nano and micro-fish category.

A group of at least six fish is strongly recommended — the species is naturally shoaling, and small numbers become skittish and show poor colouration. A 60-litre tank (60 cm footprint minimum) accommodates a species-only group comfortably; a larger tank is required if kept with tankmates.

Water temperature of 18–26 °C suits wild populations, which experience seasonal temperature swings in subtropical southeastern Australia. The species is generally considered cool-tolerant compared to most tropical aquarium fish, though sustained temperatures below 16 °C should be avoided. A heater is advisable in temperate homes to prevent cold snaps below this threshold.

Coastal and estuarine populations of P. signifer are euryhaline — they naturally move through tidal freshwater and brackish zones and tolerate salinities from near-zero to approximately 20 ppt. Aquarium husbandry for these populations benefits from slight salt additions (1–3 g/L marine salt), though pure freshwater populations from upland streams should not be subjected to brackish conditions. Commercially available stock tends to be of lowland or estuarine origin, so mild brackish conditions are generally appropriate and may reduce disease incidence.

The mouth is small; all offered foods must be appropriately fine. Surface feeding is characteristic. Floating or slow-sinking micro-pellets, crushed flake, baby brine shrimp, daphnia, and micro-worms are all accepted. Multiple small feedings daily mimic natural foraging and support the best condition.

Males are more colourful and develop extended dorsal and anal fins. Males may display and chase one another but cause little physical harm in a spacious tank with adequate females. A ratio of one male to two females reduces persistent male-to-male tension.
breedingNotes: The Pacific Blue-Eye is an egg-scatterer that deposits adhesive eggs among fine-leafed plants and Java moss. Spawning occurs readily without special conditioning once fish are well-fed and in good condition. Eggs hatch in approximately 7–10 days at 24 °C; newly hatched fry are tiny and require infusoria or commercial first-foods (such as finely powdered fry food) for the first week before graduating to baby brine shrimp nauplii. Adults will eat eggs and fry; separating a clump of moss or a spawning mop to a dedicated rearing vessel significantly improves fry survival. P. signifer is generally considered one of the easier Pseudomugil species to breed, as some others in the genus are regarded as more demanding in their water-quality requirements.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Pseudomugil signifer
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (eastern Australia, Sydney to Cape York), temperature 18–26°C, pH 6.5–8.0, hardness 5–20 dGH, adult size ~3–4 cm, shoaling group requirement (≥6), feeding notes (small invertebrates, micro-foods), euryhaline/estuarine notes, and breeding approach. Confirmed Pseudomugilidae / Atheriniformes taxonomy.

additional.name: FishBase — Pseudomugil signifer
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Pseudomugilidae, Atheriniformes), adult size (max 4 cm TL), native range (eastern Australia), temperature range, and habitat notes. Confirmed carnivore diet (small invertebrates, zooplankton).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Pacific Blue Eye Care Guide
additional.notes: Hobby care guide. Confirmed beginner-friendly designation, group size recommendation (6+), micro-food requirements, cool-temperature tolerance, tank size (minimum 10 gal / ~38 L for species only; 60 L conservative group recommendation used), and brackish tolerance notes.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Pseudomugil signifer species profile
additional.notes: UK hobby profile. Confirmed euryhaline nature of coastal populations, water parameter ranges, nano tank suitability, shoaling behaviour, and egg-scattering breeding notes. Corroborated fine-food requirement and multiple daily feedings.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Blue-Eyes: Jewels of Australia
additional.notes: TFH feature on Australian Pseudomugil species. Confirmed that P. signifer is the most commonly available and beginner-accessible Pseudomugil; noted coastal vs. upland population variation in salinity tolerance; described iridescent blue iris and male finnage display; supported 18–26°C range and brackish-tolerance of coastal ecotypes.

fish.breedingNotes: The Pacific Blue-Eye scatters adhesive eggs among fine-leafed plants or Java moss and does not guard them. Eggs hatch in roughly 7–10 days at 24 °C. Newly hatched fry are very small and should be fed infusoria or finely powdered fry food for the first week, then transitioned to baby brine shrimp nauplii. Because adults will generally eat both eggs and fry, moving a clump of moss or a spawning mop to a separate rearing vessel is the most reliable way to raise a meaningful number of young.
